content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. looking for movies about unremarkable women

**Score:** 52
**Body:** i just saw clockwatchers (1997) and i love toni collette’s character and, i guess i don’t really know how to describe it, but she’s just some woman who works in an office. not a super hero or a famous person, or even remotely interesting, but still, we follow her story and care about her and this slice of her life.

hope this makes sense to anyone & if anyone has any recs!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If you haven't already, you may want to meet one Bridget Jones, England's patron saint of being a bit of a girlfailure.
  - “You have no messages.  Not a single one.  Not even from your mother.”
2. This is something UK cinema does really well I think. 
Not just women either. 

Shirley Valentine (1989) is my Mum's favourite film. It's about a middle aged housewife who lives a boring life in a boring town, but had big wild dreams when she was young. 

Housewife 49 (2006) is based of the diaries of a housewife during WW2, the UK government had a programme called mass observation which had People complete anonymous diaries so they could help understand how the war was affecting the population. And this is based on her. 

Pat and Margaret (1994) is about the reunion of a working class woman and her long list sister who turns out to be television celebrity. 

Educating Rita (1983) is about a working class woman who starts part time university and the conflict between that and her ordinary life. 

Alan Bennet's work, dramatic plays and one person talking heads are iconic and often feature very ordinary women. 






3. Have you seen Toni Colette in Muriel's Wedding? In fact Aussie films love low key characters that aren't typically the ones who get celebrated in life. You could also see Strictly Ballroom, Babyteeth, Looking for Alibrandi and Rabbit-proof fence. The last three have girls/adolescents as central characters but it is still a female focus and they're all brilliant films. 
4. Only Yesterday (1991). It's animated, it's about an office worker who takes her vacation and spends it on a farm, where she reminisces about her childhood. Very touching, and be sure to watch through the end of the credits.
5. Welcome to the Dollhouse
6. The Good Girl (2002)
7. Da’Vine Joy Randolph’s character in The Holdovers was incredible. 

Some others:   
\+ Past Lives  
\+ Lady Bird  
\+ The Worst Person in the World  
\+ Steel Magnolias  
\+ Roma  
\+ Nomadland  
\+ The Assistant  
\+ Frances Ha  
\+ The Hours  
\+ Fish Tank  
\+ Little Women (every version)  
\+ The Joy Luck Club  
\+ Brick Lane
8. Brooklyn
9. 9 to 5

## 2. Is there a good horror movie where the horror is extremely subtle?

**Score:** 40
**Body:** I don't mean subtle as in slow. More like, you have to be looking for it to see it. It doesn't flashbang your attention to the creepy stuff like The Conjuring does, but the creepy stuff is there anyways 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Others (2001). 
2. Haunting of Hill House (series) has a ton of Easter-eggy stuff.   So subtle to 90% of folks miss it.

There is some decidedly un-subtle horror in the series too, however. 
3. The Wicker Man (1973). The horror was banal, everyday, everywhere, but just barely visible, until ....
4. The Changeling (1980)
5.  *I See You* (2019) and *Lake Mungo* (2008). And if you are open to older and black-and-white films, then *Carnival of Souls* (1962) and *The Innocents* (1961). 
6. Tár
7. This maybe isn’t what you’re looking for but I like recommending Pontypool.  It’s not that the horror is subtle but it got to me differently.  It somehow got under my skin in a way that was unexpected.  I went in blind and enjoyed the ride.
8. Parents (1988)
9. The Haunting (1963) is considered a gold standard. 
10. Caveat. My all time favorite horror 

## 3. Im looking for movies that are thinkers

**Score:** 30
**Body:** Ex: Dont Look Up, Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind, The secret Life of Walter Mitty, 500 Days of Summer. More just movies that leave you with a feeling of "i gotta think about this to get the message"

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Arrival
2. Vanilla Sky
3. Primer

And if you figure it all out, let me know. 
4. Memento

\*It doesn’t have a philosophical message to it, but it’s a “thinker” as you try to piece things together.
5. It's What's Inside 

Burning

Leave the World Behind 

Twin Peaks season 3, basically a movie.
6. Donnie Darko

Sorry To Bother You 
7. Inception.     
Requiem for a Dream.   
Donnie Darko.    
Sliding Doors
8. The Lobster (2015)

The Truman Show (1998)
9. Black swan 
10. 2001: Space Odyssey 

## 4. Looking for movies with reality distortion, survival mysteries, strange environments, and mind bending concepts

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I am looking for movie recommendations from any country and in any language.

Recently, I discovered that my favorite movies are not necessarily defined by genre, but by unusual concepts and mysterious situations. I especially enjoy stories where reality feels wrong, characters are trapped in strange circumstances, or there is an unexplained mystery that slowly unfolds.

Some elements I enjoy:

• Reality distortion and altered perceptions

• Time loops and non linear timelines

• Strange environments and liminal spaces

• Survival mysteries

• Psychological mind games

• Unexplained phenomena

• Isolated groups trying to understand what is happening

• Intelligent protagonists solving unusual problems

Movies I enjoyed that fit this feeling include:

Circle (2015), Identity (2003), The Game (1997), The Fare (2018), Old (2021), Oculus (2013), Cube (1997)

Don't Breathe, Most Dangerous Game, The Thing, Primal Fear, Predator: Killer of Killers

How to Make a Killing, V for Vendetta, The Witch Part 1 and 2, The Great Flood

Lesson of the Evil, The Exit 8

I am open to horror, thriller, mystery, science fiction, supernatural, or even other genres if they capture this type of atmosphere.

I am not looking for romance focused stories.

What would you recommend?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Coherence
2. The Cell 
3. Donnie Darko, Mulholland Drive, Dark City, and eXistenZ
4. The Thirteenth Floor
5. Color Out of Space (2019)

Caddo Lake (2024)
6. Dave Made a Maze
7. Backrooms, go see it. 
8. Not necessarily good movies, but fun concepts.

- Coherence
- Vivarium 
- Time Lapse

You've probably seen, but worth mentioning.

- Lucy
- Edge of Tomorrow
- In Time
- Arrival

TV Series

- The OA
- Dark
- Severance 

9. Mirrormask (2005)

What Dreams May Come (Robbin Williams)

Hugo (more unique than mind bending)

WristCutters: a love story (fun/ quirky/ great sound track)

Tidelands (Terry Gilliam. Pretty dark/ surreal)
10. Skinamarink and Event Horizon. Two very different films than one another, but fit your categories.

## 5. Looking for Time travel movie suggestions

**Score:** 22
**Body:** These days I have been into a lot of Time Travel stuff like I just completed watching Dark and it is genuinely mind blowing but too long I am looking for movies with similar genre and vibe which will keep me hooked. I have watched Interstellar, Looper so something around that.  
Soo thank you for the suggestions in advanced and until then waiting for some amazing movies to binge. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Primer (2004)
  - The best time travel movie I"ll never understand.
2. Timecrimes

Triangle

Predestination
3. Palm Springs is amazing although it's a time loop, not travel
4. Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die involves a time traveler 
5. 12 monkeys
6. About Time


7. Predestination - Go in blind and just watch -- 
  - I worked on that film! Great team behind it. 
8. Safety Not Guaranteed 
I have not seen it myself but very much want to. 

The Butterfly Effect- there’s a trilogy of sorts but can only recommend the first one. 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. This sequence in Civil War is easily one of the most intense scenes in modern cinema. Jesse Plemons completely owned those seven minutes and made them the best part of the movie.

**Score:** 3633
**Body:** What other 5–10 minute sequences do you think match or surpass this level of intensity?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He was onset, with their kids, while Dunst was filming. Dunst suggested he play the role and he agreed.

The red sunglasses were his touch. At first the costume designer and a few others didn’t agree, but Plemons convinced them to let him have a chance with them on. He went through his scene and they immediately understood why he wanted them.

It’s led to many fan theories, but the one I think makes the most sense is the soldier just picked the glasses  up from one of the civilians he’s murdered and simply put them on. Never thought anything of it.

What makes that scene stand out is Plemons is able to realistically, and very quickly, show us what it would be like to run into a complete and total psychopath in a situation like that. It makes us realize how very real that possibility could be.  
The blood on his hands, the red eyes, the rifle, the bodies in the background, and his cold demeanor all mean the situation has gone from bad to worse in hurry.
  - He plays a phenomenal psychopath. My wife and I nicknamed him Todd the gentle psychopath in Breaking Bad. And there is a newer one that has Emma Stone in it and he kidnaps her. The trailer makes him seem dangerously insane too. 
    - I'd recommend Bugonia if you haven't seen it yet. Jesse Plemons and Emma Stone are so good in it and I'm not typically an Emma Stone fan.
2. Even more sinister than when he confronted his neighbors for not being invited in Game Night.  
![gif](giphy|26wkSP3tzHKqHNlAc)
  - He's my favorite part of that movie, I didn't really know much about him as an actor but I was captivated.

I love this scene:

**Gary:** "Three bags of Tostito's Scoops, I notice."

**Max:** "There was a special on these tonight. Three for one."

**Gary:** "Three for one?"

**Max:** "Yep."

**Gary:** "How can that be profitable for Frito Lay?"

**Max:** "These corporations, I don't know what they're doing."

**Gary:** "Well, you two enjoy each other. It's often we don't appreciate what we have until it's gone."

https://youtu.be/bYM6tWIjr-I
    - underrated movie, & he is the best part of it. 
3. And what’s crazy is you can insert basically any political or national issue/topic for what he’s saying and it can still be the same intensity 
4. Its a classic, but Joe Pesci asking "What do you mean I'm funny?" The whole atmosphere immediately changes and in that moment, on first watch,  you've no idea how its going to pan out.
5. The coin scene in No Country for Old Men pops to mind 
6. Its definitely his glasses. They are in stark contrast with his uniform. Makes him unpredictable. Thats what makes it intense. 

## 2. Tom Hanks Says the Oscars Don’t Need a Voice Acting Category Because Voice Performances Should Be Able to Win Best Actor and Actress

**Score:** 824

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. And what? They share the Oscar with the people who animated the movements? Think tom hanks, think. 
  - What will you have in 500 years? 
    - ![gif](giphy|VolkaaMsGgwHSAS1nd)
2. I love Tom Hanks but he is barking up the wrong tree here.
  - Yeah the facial expressions and movement of the actor all play a big part. Even mocap gives license to the effects artist to alter the performance 
3. Um, no. It's a different skill and a different medium. Since animated films are included it would make sense for voice actors to have their own genderless category. The Emmys have a category of "Outstanding Character Voice-Over Performance" for voice actors.
  - But he’s clearly correct! Look at all the voice actors who have already won! Wait, none have won? Oh jeez. Well, at least there are dozens who have been nominated—it’s only a matter of time. Wait, none have *ever* been nominated? Oh man. Maybe Tom Hanks is full of shit. 
4. He's entirely wrong, unfortunately.
5. Never really considered it but it would be funny if Hollywood started to produce animated Oscar bait haha
6. ![gif](giphy|ceeN6U57leAhi)



## 3. One of the realest lines in film:

**Score:** 820
**Body:** *Superbad* (2007)

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/2iggl7sj1y6h1.jpeg?width=600&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=f42cf976f4737d90c7132acae6bd942624bc1ef1
  - "I have game bro"
  - For like a decade this line always got a laugh because people got the reference. I dropped it at work a couple months ago and crickets. 
    - How was that HR meeting? 
      - "So, yeah, you know...we had a mutually beneficial parting of the ways..."
2. Superbad was a great documentary on the finite nature of youth. 

“My back is located on my cock” refers to unprecedented flexibility of the young. 
3. I love this movie. It’s timeless. 
4. This is why it's so easy for gay men to get sum fux.
5. "Why don't you go piss your pants again?"
  - That was like 8 years ago ass hole!

*People don't forget*

## 4. Out of all the Wes Anderson’s movies, The Grand Budapest Hotel is still his best movie

**Score:** 642
**Body:** The cast, writing, story, music, costumes, and even the setting made this movie really good. Wes Anderson is a pretty good director.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. By far , it's a masterpiece 
  - I find his movies very hit-and-miss. Some of them are just style over substance, and I'm getting a little tired of it. But Grand Budapest Hotel is so damn good. It's easily Anderson's best for me.
2. ![gif](giphy|jsTIwpUm3fE82EULPK)


3. ![gif](giphy|VS3zCR5S6frDW)
  - ![gif](giphy|yisTEcy0Bf56w)  
Listen, I’m not dying, but I need some time
4. Royal Tenenbaums is top notch 
  - Tenenbaums is the real MVP
5. ![gif](giphy|ljaDzjv8cpIpa)
6. Idk I would say fantastic Mr. Fox is in contention
  - ![gif](giphy|13Xy3MWV2Psz4I)

## 5. Would you recommend St. Elmo's Fire? (I haven't seen it yet)

**Score:** 337
**Body:** I've never seen this movie, I really like the soundtrack and also the actors and era. Could you recommend it? 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's like Breakfast Club only with adults. They are all friends from college. You see them navigate their young adulthood in the early 80s. 
  - My mom imagines they are like a trilogy.  The Breakfast Club, then St. Elmo's Fire, then The Big Chill.
2. Absolute Cinema

![gif](giphy|yr7n0u3qzO9nG)
3. Is this the one where Demi Moore locks the apartment door and everyone freaks out and tries to break in and save her while I'm trying to find the remote and change the channel to something good?
  - Yes
  - Yeah, this movie was pretty silly but trying to be serious. I kept watching hoping something good would happen.
4. All I know is the song is an absolute banger

[https://youtu.be/dx7vNdAb5e4?is=kJPJtl5-hz2VLDMr](https://youtu.be/dx7vNdAb5e4?is=kJPJtl5-hz2VLDMr)
5. I hated it back then and haven’t watched it since. I remember being both bored and annoyed.
6. I loved it growing up, I was like 15 growing up. 

But it didn’t age well, there are a few relationships that are toxic and/or stalkerish that were romanticized. But it has its moments, great young cast and an awesome soundtrack. 
7. I like this film for pretty much the exact reasons people hate it.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. A year before Oldboy, Park Chan-wook spent the 2002 World Cup in hiding — his essay «The sin of not liking soccer» was just translated into English for the first time

**Score:** 218
**Body:** Park describes confessing it like an actual sin: his first time in church in twenty years, working up the courage to tell the priest «I… I don't like soccer.»

His agony was badly timed. Korea, co-hosting, improbably reached fourth place, and with every goal the entire country celebrated, Park fell deeper into despair — he compares himself to a *chinilpa*, a collaborator in hiding after the empire has fallen: «One night, I had a nightmare in which I screamed 'I can't stand the World Cup' at the top of my lungs and subsequently got my mouth disfigured.»

A year later he made Oldboy: a man trapped, alone, in circumstances he cannot comprehend, who resolves it through violent rampage. Make of the timing what you will.

Find the translation here: [https://mubi.com/en/notebook/posts/translation-the-sin-of-not-liking-soccer](https://mubi.com/en/notebook/posts/translation-the-sin-of-not-liking-soccer)

(We came across it writing The Footnotes, a daily World Cup diary from the European Review of Books — and we extend our deepest sympathies to Park after South Korea's comeback win this morning.)

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That's genuinely hilarious. Tbf the state of the current sports is ridiculous. Purely profit driven commercial event that exposes the inequality and corrupt politics. Iran team getting the 48h visa 💀
  - You’re spot on, though I’d also add that sports dislikers (not the sports indifferent) can be so dramatic about their dislike. As someone who’s been in circles where sports are small to nonexistent parts of the cultures, I can’t tell how many times I’ve heard people aggressively mock sports or mock people who like sports.

Once witnessed a woman rage over a newspaper article that called Michael Jordan “poetry in motion.” It was a massive affront to poetry she would not stand for.

Like could you imagine if I said “you like movies?! So weird that people are into movies”? But “sports ball” mockery is very real.
    - Ok! Well, as a book review, we feel professionally obliged to step in there: calling Jordan «poetry in motion» is unfair to Jordan himself. Poems make you pause, Jordan made you jump
      - I can see the smoke pouring out of her ears.
    - Sure, sports culture is a joke but sports in itself isn't. Sports is natural
      - Entertainment culture gave rise to paparazzi and a billion tabloids that have journalists airing intimate details of people’s private lives. 
2. Can't stand is not the same as not liking. 

It appears that Park really hated the national sport. 😂

While I dont hate it, I never understood why people spend so much time watching games on TV. 

But I suppose they could ask me why do I spend so much time watching movies.
  - The national sport of Korea isn't football, it's taekwondo.
3. I always found the villain's lack of true motive or justification to be the biggest gut-punch of the film, even moreso than the familial relationship thing. It's not *totally* unprompted, but it's such an outsized response to being "wronged" that you realize it really wasn't about the main character and all the time he/the audience spent searching for the reason was pointless.

The idea of the villain being a stand-in for the (to Park, inexplicable) overriding enthusiasm for soccer is pretty funny to me.
  - Yeah, football not owing anyone a motive either, it locks you in for the month and waits, it's a perfect villain.

## 2. Nymphomaniac: Organizing a Life Around Lack

**Score:** 175
**Body:** The first time I watched *Nymphomaniac* ten years ago, my reaction was simple: this director must be insane.  
What stayed with me wasn’t just the sex or the provocation. It was a kind of honesty that felt almost hostile. The film seemed determined to push past every comforting illusion people normally use to make sense of themselves.  
Over the years, I’ve found myself returning to it whenever my anxiety gets particularly bad. Strangely, it never makes me feel worse. If anything, it works like a form of psychic pornography—a strangely addictive source of comfort.  
The older I get, the less I think *Nymphomaniac* is really about sex.  
From a Lacanian perspective, Joe’s problem isn’t excessive desire. It’s what happens when desire collapses into drive.  
As long as desire has an object, there is still the fantasy that satisfaction might be possible. But once that fantasy begins to break down, something else emerges. The pursuit continues, yet the object itself becomes increasingly irrelevant.  
This is exactly what happens to Joe.  
As the film progresses, the men matter less and less. Pleasure itself matters less and less. She moves from one encounter to another, only to discover that what she is looking for is never there.  
Because she was never really looking for a particular person.  
She is searching for something that cannot quite be named.  
Something already lost.  
It feels as though wherever she arrives, lack has arrived first.  
Seen this way, *Nymphomaniac* is not a film about excess. It is a film about the impossibility of satisfaction.  
One scene that has always fascinated me is the morgue sequence.  
Many viewers find it grotesque or absurd. I’ve always thought it was one of the film’s most revealing moments.  
Sex has been stripped of almost everything we normally associate with it. There is no romance, no seduction, no mutual recognition. The object is literally dead, yet the movement continues.  
What remains is no longer desire but drive.  
Joe’s actions no longer aim at fulfillment. They simply continue.  
The film’s more disturbing moments—the morgue, the masochism, the self-destruction—are often dismissed as provocation. But I think they are better understood through Lacan’s concept of *jouissance*.  
Jouissance is not pleasure. It is the strange form of enjoyment that persists beyond pleasure, often taking the form of pain, repetition, and self-destruction.  
The subject does not keep returning because it feels good.  
The subject returns because something in them cannot stop.  
For me, however, the most unsettling thing in the film is neither the sex nor the violence.  
It is Joe’s refusal to become normal.  
She refuses to become a repaired person.  
And that refusal touches on something deeply uncomfortable:  
If the symptom disappears, who am I without it?  
This is what makes the line “The secret of sex is love” so interesting.  
In von Trier’s world, love is not the solution to desire.  
If anything, love begins where the fantasy of satisfaction ends.  
Love is not the union of two complete people. It is the recognition that neither person is complete.  
Lacan famously described love as giving what one does not have.  
Love emerges not from fullness, but from lack.  
Not from possession, but from the acknowledgment that possession is impossible.  
In that sense, *Nymphomaniac* is not really about sex at all.  
It is about the ways human beings organize themselves around something that can never be fully resolved.  
More than any of the sexual scenes, I find myself thinking about the tree.  
When Joe recognizes the barren tree on the cliff as her soul tree, the film suddenly becomes quiet.  
There is no climax. No redemption. No narrative of growth.  
Only recognition.  
Throughout the film, Joe searches for herself through objects, experiences, and other people. Here, for the first time, the search stops.  
What she recognizes is not merely a tree.  
It is the part of herself that will never disappear.  
It is not beautiful.  
It is not whole.  
It cannot be healed by love, corrected by growth, or erased by time.  
Yet it remains.  
Like a wound.  
Or perhaps like the soul itself.  
Lacan would call this dimension the Real: that which resists being fully symbolized, explained, or understood.  
The Real is not an answer.  
It is a rupture.  
It does not disappear.  
The task is not to overcome it, but to continue living in its presence.  
This is why I don’t think *Nymphomaniac* is really a film about sex addiction or moral decline.  
It is about what remains after desire fails.  
What fascinates me most about Lars von Trier is the almost brutal honesty of his gaze.  
He keeps asking questions long after most people are satisfied with the answers.  
When others stop at desire, he asks about the lack beneath desire.  
When others stop at trauma, he asks about the enjoyment hidden within trauma.  
When others rush toward meaning, he remains obsessed with questions that may never be answered.  
I don’t know if *Nymphomaniac* has taught me anything.  
What I feel instead is a profound sense of recognition.  
As though someone had reached that place before me.  
He did not lead me out of the forest.  
He simply left a few lights behind.  
Enough to let me know that someone had truly been there.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Your reading of the film makes me want to watch it quite a bit, because I think this is a fairly profound philosophical position in regards to drive...passing well beneath Lacan to perhaps Spinoza (who reads everything in terms of Drive in this sense), or even some aspects of Buddhism...but, honestly, why do I want to watch 3 hrs of Drive? What does this do for you? I'm quite frankly worried about being bored by it all. 
  - Honestly it's quite entertaining imo. The whole thing is 5 hours long actually, but divided in two parts. There is no rule saying that you need to watch everything on one sitting. Taking a nreal between parts 1 and 2 will make you appreciate the film better.
    - It's just a fear I have about the film/s. Same thing that kept me away from Pasolini's 120 Days as well. In the past I've found Lars von Trier's films a little bit flat, like I'm being flatly drawn into an disturbed intensity, like in Antichrist or Melancholia, both of which nearly completely erased themselves from my mind after seeing them (I can hardly remember a frame). I feel like I'm kind of "working" while watching.

What is it that you found so entertaining? People find different things entertaining.
      - Well, personally I like how it is structured. It feels like an anthology of mini stories of sorts. The protagonist is the same, but each chapter is a small, almost self contained tale about her experiences with sex and lust, with her present self narrating and a priest interjecting. The two of them debate over the events unfolding on screen, so there is always this push and pull of ideas moving things forward. I personally like those devices that von Trier used here, and I feel they make the film feel much more dynamic than much of his previous work. (By the way, The House That Jack Built uses the same narrative devices again, but instead of lust, the sin being examined is murder.)
        - Very cool description. I appreciate you taking the time to relate that.
      - I will say up front that his films have always left me with some of the most indelible images, and so I don’t know if I will successfully speak to you where you currently are. 

But I find his movies more personable and engaging, with more relatable substance to hang on to, than many “art films.” He has a good sense of black humor, a very strong visual and aesthetic sensibility, and a firm and compelling artistic take on the phenomena of life. He is like a funnier, subversive, pitch-black Terrence Malick to me. Antichrist is basically a reclamation of nature for the forces of pain and grief, Melancholia is his (absent) god movie, and Nymphomaniac is his To the Wonder. He is playing with all the raw materials of human life Malick sweeps under the rug.
        - Yeah, not really a Terrance Malick person either, I'm afraid I may have really made a mistake having just ordered Nymphomania 1 and 2 (!!! hahahaha, we will see). Sometimes you drag yourself to a film and it surprises you.
      - I find it quite extraordinary that you barely remember Melancholia, which is a film that is quite reputed for its memorable shots and imagery. I’m not criticizing, I’m genuinely puzzled.
2. Having just watched this recently I thought it was pretty obvious that it wasn't about nymphomania per se, but a study on addiction in general, and the journey through the stages of worsening addiction. He also takes shots at the system and culture of substance abuse recovery and treatment in a few places.

Seligman serves as a take-down of film criticism and also of just overanalysis of films. She literally cusses him out on a few occasions about not just letting her tell the story instead of trying to shoehorn the fibonacci sequence "crap" in at every opportunity, telling him that the story would be better if he'd just shut up and believe in it. 

Most of his interjections seem to show us what a film would be like if the audience was blatantly explained every bit of nuance and symbolism instead of making us find it ourselves. There's actually quite a lot of comedy in the Seligman scenes despite the film seeming like a very dark piece.

HOWEVER, another part of Seligman's character is personifying the typical attitude towards addicts. During the first half she has to fire back at him more than once when he won't let her call herself a bad person for doing bad things to others in the process of chasing her highs. He's displaying the societal attitude of blaming the addiction as if the addict has no free will and control and should be absolved of shame. He keeps trying to rationalize the impacts of her behavior instead of allowing her to process the moral implications of what she's done. She insists that she should be facing her guilt and shame, not just absloving herself due to the addiction. (The truth IMO is probably somewhere in the middle.)

Von Trier famously stated that he wrote under the influence and feared loss of creativity without drugs and alcohol. He was also notably sober when he tackled Nymphomaniac. And he had attended AA before, and seems to show us his opinion of that treatment modality when Joe attends some twelve step meetings.

There's multiple themes going on, and your takes have merit within her mental state. But the overall main theme struck me as being addiction in general, how it impacts an individual's life and the lives of everyone around them, and how it progresses in severity.

The work struck me as a very personal study on addiction for von Trier, much as Melancholia was a very personal take on depression for him and Dunst.

I really loved Melancholia too, one of the best total sensory experiences I've seen in film. I haven't seen Antichrist yet but it sounds like it might cover grief and trauma like Melancholia and Nymphomaniac seem to cover depression and addiction in this unofficial "trilogy" of mental health films.
3. Did you watch the director's cut? It contains the abortion scene and during the following conversation it's clearer than ever that Joe is just a stand-in for Trier, arguing his points.

## 3. The Thing (1982): The ending only makes sense thematically if both of them are human

**Score:** 53
**Body:** From the beginning, MacReady assigned himself as the de-facto leader of the whole group, because he's the self-proclaimed most levelheaded. But at the beginning of the movie, with the chess game, we saw that he takes things to extremes and can act irrationally. He's self destructive, which is only made worse by all the paranoia throughout the film.

So the movie ends just like MacReady's chess game at the beginning of the movie; he 'beats' the computer by pouring his drink onto it which destroys it, and he beats the alien by destroying the base. In both cases, he won but at the cost of his own defeat. He's also indirectly responsible for 5/10 deaths. He isolates Blair, he leaves Fuchs to do research alone, he splits Naul and Garry up to arm the bombs, and he kills Clark. Clearly his leadership skills were not the greatest.

And so that final scene only really works if both of them are human. Throughout that whole scene it's clear that MacReady is still very distrusting of Childs; even handing him the drink seemed like a calculated move to test him. His ending line "*Why don't we just wait here a little while...see what happens*" expresses this. He still doesn't trust Childs, even though he himself said that neither of them are "*in much shape to do anything about it*", and he's gonna die soon no matter what. If he had the strength, I think he would've outright killed Childs just to be safe. Not to mention that Childs was left alone seemingly because MacReady, as leader, ordered him to remain at the base while the three of them checked on Blair (for some reason).

Meanwhile, Childs has no doubts that MacReady could've been assimilated. He immediately trusts that he's human. Accepting the drink shows that trust and his resignation of his paranoia. He's found peace with his fate, which is exemplified by his last line of dialogue: "*What do we do?*" Throughout the whole movie he's been at odds with MacReady, constantly questioning his decisions and resisting his leadership. But in the end, he's able to listen to his reason and even share a drink with him with no hard feelings, despite them having a lot of animosity for each other at the start of the film.

If Childs were assimilated in the end then MacReady's self-destructive paranoia was right, which I don't think is what the movie wants to say. The group's distrust of each other is what ultimately led to their demises. If they had stuck together, like they knew they should, most of them probably would've been able to find a way to defeat the alien and survive.

(And I don't think there's any possibility that MacReady was the one assimilated because he continued fighting the alien even after he believed he was the last man standing, so there was no reason to keep pretending.)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I agree...partially. It's a bit of a stretch to say "the group's distrust of each other is what ultimately led to their demises." It's the group's distrust *and* the shapeshifting man-eating monster they're trapped with. That's where the tension that makes the movie comes from. They both need to be alive to the possibility that at least one of them is not who he says he is *and* stick together.

So I think the movie absolutely wants you to sit with the ambiguity at the end. So you're probably right, they are both human, but what makes the ending tragic is that neither of them, based on everything they've seen, is exactly wrong to distrust the other. We know you get assimilated when you're out on your own, and both of them have been out on their own for at least some time.
  - I've always thought that any interpretation that definitively removes the ambiguity defuses the film's power to some degree. With a definitive answer we can sit and judge events back from a safe epistemic position, whereas when we sit with the ambiguity we're forced to occupy the same paranoid mindset as the characters picking apart little details that may or may not be clues and unsure who to trust.
  - Yep. The only real brightside is that they may have succeeded in dealing with the threat. But as they mention to each other, once the fire dies down, they're going to freeze to death anyway.

The nearest base already suffered the same fate. Help isn't coming. And the unnerving thing they still both have to think about is whether the Thing's really dead or not.
2. I like this and I agree I think they’re both human at the end, but I think the theme of a lingering paranoia between them is part of what the movie is trying to say. 

The ordeal is so traumatic that they’ll never shake the feeling that either could be assimilated.
  - Yep, the choice to have a black man and a white man be the last two standing, having a deep distrust of one another, is not a coincidence. They really should be “in this together” as fellow humans, but the fact neither feels completely safe around the other is a pretty powerful way of showing division within the society.
3. On the point of MacReady continuing to fight the alien, as my understanding goes it is unclear if it is possible the person being copied completely is entirely aware they have been copied, whether it suddenly takes over or if it just alters how they think etc.

Additionally on his leadership skills, he is not the leader, he is the helicopter pilot, he only becomes the leader by essentially threatening to use the flamethrower on anyone who disagreed so.... not the best first step lol.
4. I think you're off the mark in the first paragraph: hes not acting irrationally by destroying the computer if he thinks he can never win. It is oerfectly rational to not play if you thing a game sucks. Likewise, the only way to beat a perfect parasitic organism is to burn it all down. 
5. There's massive evidence that Childs is the Thing. (Video game canon be damned, no one counts it). MaCready lost the chess game to an illegal move which completely mimics how he was playing a game with The Thing the whole movie with an unfair advantage. Offering Childs the drink at the end is completely synonymous with the chess game in the beginning when he "offers" the computer a drink for winning. MaCready is offering him a drink since he knew he lost the "game." This is a well accepted theory among the fandom

The coat changes color in the room Childs was in when MacCready/Nauls/Gary were in his shack. He had to put a new one on when the Thing infected him (see next videos)

The door to the right of Childs' lookout spot is closed one scene, then it is open in the next when he disappears. The shot of the camera going around the base while it's empty points towards the stairs (where The Thing currently is before it shuts off the generator). The Thing snuck through the storage room to his right, then opened it to attack him. (see vid: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZmaOP1SgFHo). Here's a section of a video that explains what I just typed (https://youtu.be/hF2hHhVy9aU?si=ja_SgLC7Yb_h9hYh&t=2227)

It makes zero sense why Childs even ran out in the first place. The Thing attacked and assimilated him then went down to the basement to break the generator which was shut off not 5 seconds after Childs ran away. The only way to the generator room would be to go passed Childs since his lookout room is directly next to the stairs leading to the generator room.

This is a theory, but makes sense in the context of the characters. The Thing doesn't want to assimilate MaCready at all since it sees him as an outsider and doesn't want to inherit his personality. Every other character in the movie likes to socialize, but MaCready stays away from everyone in his shack, even sleeping there while everyone is in the base. It sees MaCready as an impulsive and reckless hothead that it doesn't want to become like. (this is a theory again but it makes a lot of sense when you see how different MaCready is from everyone else on rewatches)

Also, if we care about director's statements years later, Carpenter directly says (in 2012) that one of them is the Thing (via twitter; obviously can only be Childs)
6. I disagree when the creature can be any living thing, the paranoia is the point. The fact that they don't know how transmission works with the creature so Childs took the offer of a drink says everything we need to know to accept how we think it should end. Either the creature is intelligent enough to play the spy game and pretend to be paranoid and not take a drink or Childs has been turned and turning others is top priority. But the paranoia is key, it's a Tinker, Tailor, Soldier, Spy

## 4. Manhunter shows how criminally underrated a movie can be?

**Score:** 50
**Body:** I generally avoid movies based on serial killers, but I am truly an avid admirer of this movie upon watching it.

Manhunter is a classic in its own right. Everything from the aesthetic and atmosphere to the music and direction is executed beautifully. 

The pacing is relentless, and the tension is so palpable that you cannot look away. Trust me, you wouldn't want to.

It’s an incredibly subtextual screenplay where every Chekhov's gun fires exactly when needed. Reba's blindness is shown beautifully without ever being explicitly mentioned, the fatal bullets are perfectly foreshadowed in the second act, and the absurdism of the Tattler magazine is captured flawlessly.

Michael Mann is nocturnal by nature. He thrives in the dark, utilizing neon signs, street lights, deep isolation, and pale blue moonlight. But here, he deliberately starts and ends the movie with blinding sunlight, a beach, and an unfathomable ocean. It proves Mann had total, uncompromising control over his own signature style, pushing it to an entirely new level.

Michael Mann is a master of visual atmosphere, and the way he portrayed it here deserves immense praise.

What impressed me most is the absolute efficiency of the blueprint. There are absolutely no filler scenes. The script drops you directly into the inciting incident with a hero who is scarred for life, and it closes brilliantly on a happier note, yet with that exact same hero, permanently scarred. 

The whole cast did their job so convincingly, surprisingly grounded for a thriller of this kind. 

William Petersen grounds the entire film. The way he transitions from calm to terrified and vulnerable in a matter of seconds shows the massive range of his performance.

Opposite him, Tom Noonan is genuinely, deeply scary, and Mann captures the crushing isolation of all these characters perfectly.

And Brian Cox plays Hannibal Lector (spelled Lecktor in the movie for some reason) so convincingly that he completely steals the show in just the two scenes he has. It walked so Silence of The Lambs could run, where Anthony Hopkins won the Oscar for one of the shortest screen times in cinema history as Hannibal. I guess there's something especially terrifying about Hannibal's character, and both actors chose their roles so perfectly.

And the greatest thing about this movie was how unsettling it was without being obvious. There are no severed hands, chopped bodies, pools of blood, or torture machines like you see in typical psychopath movies. Yet I'd count this among the most disturbing movies I've seen. Just the performances and atmosphere alone would make your saliva evaporate in the third act. The buildup crashes down so slowly, and the rollercoaster lands so smoothly.

My only real gripe is the climax. The shootout felt a bit unrealistic, as if Mann briefly let go of the grounded tension he spent two hours building. But for an ’80s moviegoer, it probably wouldn't have been a problem. Ultimately, the visual atmosphere alone cements this as a masterpiece.

If Letterboxd had an option for the top 5 movies list, Manhunter definitely would have been there for me. But why complain? It STILL is in my top 5.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. agree with every point. The very realistic maximum security facility, it's sterile, soundless brutality was much more terrifying than the somewhat silly dungeon depicted in the other movie. Brian Cox was also very believable. I recall an alternate ending which I'm not sure is on the dvd or was just on cable was chilling and revealing more about Peterson's character. It was one of the first DVD's I bought.
2. It's far, far better than Silence, in my humble opinion, and one of the best crime movies I've ever seen.

Tom Noonan is so good and so unsettling and Brian Cox is still the one I think of when someone mentions the Hannibal character.
3. Ive always loved Manhunter and know it shot by shot at this point. The soundtrack is great. The one bit that irks me is at the climactic shootout, the frame rate slows while Dolarhyde rips through the wallpaper, then theres some really dodgy editing when he lifts his shotgun to the window, like you see it twice from two different cuts. It's like someone really rushed editing the ending. 
4. The opening to this movie haunts me. Every tiny noise I hear at night makes me think that a flashlight is about to shine onto my bed.

And now, some random thoughts to meet word count requirements: Tom Noonan is underrated. William Peterson is overrated. The movie is R-rated.

## 5. What is the Backrooms actually doing to Mary in Kane Parsons’ film?

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Most of the discussion I’ve seen regarding the ending of Backrooms (2026) lands on the darker interpretation, that Mary’s smile means she’s been claimed by the Backrooms, or that it’s an ironic kind of acceptance that she’ll be trapped at ASYNC forever just like how her mother was institutionalized. But I think that reading misses what the film was actually building toward with her character the whole time.

Mary’s opening monologue is about experiencing your life through glass, watching yourself from a distance. Her entire career as a therapist is basically a professionalized version of that. She arguably grew up in her own version of the Backrooms. Her mother’s paranoia meant sealed windows, no outside world, a childhood spent in a house that functioned like an endless interior with no real exit. She became a therapist to solve a problem she lived inside as a kid and never fully resolved.

The Backrooms is a literal manifestation of what she’s been diagnosing in other people her whole career. Infinite rooms, circular pathways, the same doors opening onto nothing new; cycles of behaviour with no apparent way out. But at the same time, the Backrooms is a place of endless generation. It’s not just a trap: it’s a place that offers a path forward if you’re willing to move through it rather than settle into it. The difference between Mary and Clark is exactly that.

My interpretation is that The Backrooms ends up giving her something she’d been circling her entire career without reaching. Once rendered in the Backrooms, the claustrophobic windowless room she grew up in now has a doorway out. The loops, the cycles, the fixed pathways, they don’t have to be permanent. But the only way through them is to actually confront what’s driving them, and that confrontation has to be genuine and not clinical. Clark couldn’t do it. Shaped by a childhood that forced her to learn how to survive inside suffocating spaces, Mary finds the gap in the wall and moves through it. She even escapes Pirate Clark using a memory from her childhood, destroying it and shattering her fixation on the past.

As Phil explains the Backrooms to her as a series of endless doors opening, his voice gradually fades out as she smiles. Her smile reads to me as someone who finally found the answer she’d been looking for, by living through the thing she’d only ever observed from a professional distance.

ASYNC might not let her leave, but does that matter to her now? She’s experienced something that has permanently reshaped her. And her Still Life shows the Backrooms have been permanently imprinted by her as well. I think she might even want to continue working with them.

Anyone else interpreted it this way? I initially felt the ambiguous ending left Mary’s character underbaked, but this really brought the film together for me.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I interpreted the ending as something of a meta moment where we see scenes from the film we just watched which now exist in the Backrooms, not just things that Mary saw or experienced (e.g. the neighbourhood where the two young shop employees live) because we the audience now have a distorted memory of those moments. Even if the Mary in this story escaped, the very fact we have experienced a moment where she was terrified or trapped in this film has created a version of Mary from that scene that will forever be in there. Once something happens, even if we "move on" in our real lives, that moment will exist and linger in the backrooms forever, which I found to be a novel spin on the downer horror movie ending.
  - This is an interesting take I hadn’t considered. I think overall I like the idea that even though the Backrooms has permanently rendered her traumatic memories, Mary still experienced meaningful change by passing through. The Backrooms will hold on to her memories, but she no longer has to
    - yeah I think it's both kind of liberating that the "real" Mary can genuinely escape and leave that stuff behind, but also terrifying that there is a version of her that can never leave, like trauma that could potentially be rediscovered again if she ever walked into the wrong room or became curious about revisiting it. 

In a way, if we the viewers were to ever re-watch the film (or even just remember it, or discuss it as we're doing now), we would be doing exactly that, knowing what we're going to find but revisiting this same horrible scenario out of curiosity, fascination or some kind of enjoyment from what we experience.
  - Super-insightful thoughts! So good to know that some people "get it." 
2. Exhuming her memories is my thinking.

My favorite visual bit is the vertical pan showing her childhood home being stripped of any semblance of her understanding of it, leaving a growing, blackhole-esque doorway. 

It’s trying to get at a few things about breaking routines as therapy and purposeful, forward movement as overcoming traumas in life but that piece of direction feels like it’s saying that trying to strip away the things that make you singular kinda empties you out. Even the traumas of your life. 
  - That vertical pan is, without a doubt, the easiest layup a marketing department has ever gotten for cutting a teaser trailer. 

They need add nothing but title/release date at the end
3. I like this interpretation. It was obvious to me how the Backrooms connect to Clark's psyche - failed architect haunted by shitty furniture finds impossible architecture filled with supernaturally shitty furniture - and I didn't have a good similar interpretation of how it connected to Mary's psyche, so I like this. 
4. The simple answer is Mary is trapped in the ASYNC facility. If you have seen the YouTube shorts then you will know that they never let people go back to society or the real world again after coming back from the Backrooms. Similarly, what they did to Peter Tench in the YouTube shorts. ASYNC is the most evil corporation that you can ever find.
  - Maybe the shorts settle this, but I like how (in the movie at least) the final scenes never make it clear whether she’s actually still in the Backrooms (just an ASYNC “colonized” space within the Backrooms) or not. I believe there’s one line where the guy says “how did you get here?” and another line where he refers to her having been “there” — both ambiguous.
5. Excellent thoughts, thank you! The  Backrooms are sentient but very flawed. They mine minds and attempt to re-create what they find there, but do a very poor job of it, as they were designed with architectural applications in mind, or rather, the tech that created them had that intent (per the Web series.) A little like how The Shimmer in "Annihilation" or Solaris in both versions of that story interact with the human psyche, but because the aren't human, and because they come with inherent flaws as humans also do, sinister results occur. (There's a direct reference to "Annihilation" in the Web series that I haven't seen anyone comment on yet, but that's for another discussion.) The "still life" figure of Mary seen at the end isn't Mary. It's a bad re-creation, an unreasonable facsimile. This leaves it to the viewer to speculate on whether she escaped, or was consumed or killed by interfacing with the Backrooms. It's been frustrating to see so many negative criticisms of the film from people who aren't really thinking about it beyond what's on the surface. But then, this film is not for everyone, even if some of the themes really are.
